title: Day 5 · Bash + Permission Engine
subtitle: This map focuses on Day 5's harness boundaries: every tool_use becomes a PermissionRequest, the permission engine returns allow / ask / deny, and bash, ask_user_question, and background jobs all pass through the same gate.

diagrams.label: Permission gate
diagrams.title: Every tool passes through one permission gate
diagrams.summary: Day 5 turns Day 4's edit-only interceptor into a general permission gate: the model only proposes a tool_use, and agent.py wraps it into PermissionRequest before tools.run.

nodes.label: tool_use
nodes.detail: The model only names a tool and arguments. It cannot directly run shell commands, write files, or access the outside network.

nodes.label: PermissionRequest
nodes.detail: The Agent Loop wraps each tool_use into PermissionRequest, including the current cwd and permission_mode.

nodes.label: decide_permission
nodes.detail: This is Day 5's core boundary: read-only tools, dangerous bash, ask tools, and all three permission modes are routed here.

nodes.label: PermissionDecision
nodes.detail: The permission engine only returns a verdict. Executing tools, asking for confirmation, or returning an error observation still happens in the Agent Loop.

nodes.label: prompt_ui
nodes.detail: This general ask branch renders command previews, diffs, or network-access confirmation. ask_user_question uses a separate menu branch and does not reach tools.run.

nodes.label: ToolRegistry.run
nodes.detail: The harness calls the real tool function only on the allow branch, or after the ask branch receives explicit confirmation.

nodes.label: error ToolResult
nodes.detail: Dangerous commands, write attempts in plan mode, and rejected confirmations all become is_error=True observations for the model.

nodes.label: ToolResult
nodes.detail: Tool results are appended back into messages, so the model can only continue from the observation.

diagrams.label: Bash safety
diagrams.title: How bash moves from preview to execution
diagrams.summary: bash is a high-risk tool: dangerous commands are blocked by regex first; normal commands show a command preview and only reach bash_runner.run_sync after y.

nodes.label: bash tool_use
nodes.detail: The model only proposes a shell string and arguments. background=True changes execution style, not the permission policy.

nodes.label: _DANGEROUS_PATTERNS
nodes.detail: The permission engine checks dangerous-command patterns before any confirmation UI. A match denies the command immediately.

nodes.label: deny ToolResult
nodes.detail: For example, git push --force origin main short-circuits to an error observation and never shows a command preview.

nodes.label: confirm_command
nodes.detail: Normal bash still asks for confirmation in default and acceptEdits modes. acceptEdits affects file edits, not shell commands.

nodes.label: bash_runner.run_sync
nodes.detail: The harness tool function actually runs shell. It uses a minimal env, pins cwd to the project, enforces timeout, and truncates output.

nodes.label: child process
nodes.detail: The command result is collected as text. Non-zero exits include the exit code; long output is shortened by truncate_output.

nodes.label: ToolResult
nodes.detail: The model sees an observation, not direct terminal control. Any test analysis or follow-up command starts from this result.

diagrams.label: Permission modes
diagrams.title: How --permission-mode changes decisions
diagrams.summary: Day 5 modes are hard constraints, not prompt hints: _ASK_TOOLS ask first; default asks for writes, acceptEdits only skips file-edit confirmation, and plan denies writes plus local execution.

nodes.label: CLI Runtime
nodes.detail: The CLI passes default / acceptEdits / plan into run_agent, then into each PermissionRequest.

nodes.label: decide_permission
nodes.detail: Permission mode takes effect here. The code checks _ASK_TOOLS before the plan branch, so ask_user_question can still ask in plan mode.

nodes.label: _ASK_TOOLS
nodes.detail: _ASK_TOOLS returns ask before the plan check. ask_user_question opens a menu; web_fetch / web_search tell the user the Agent wants network access.

nodes.label: prompt_ui
nodes.detail: This branch is not write permission. ask_user_question constructs ToolResult directly; web tools run only after user confirmation.

nodes.label: _READONLY_TOOLS
nodes.detail: The read-only allowlist can run directly in all three modes. It stays separate from _ASK_TOOLS so network access is not treated as local read-only work.

nodes.label: default
nodes.detail: In default mode, non-read-only and non-dangerous writes or local execution go through ask. Only confirmation can move them to tools.run.

nodes.label: acceptEdits
nodes.detail: acceptEdits only skips the file-edit confirmation UI. Day 4 checks still run: read-before-edit, mtime conflicts, and unique replacement.

nodes.label: plan
nodes.detail: plan mode denies writes and local execution, including file_write, file_edit, bash, and local write tools; _ASK_TOOLS already returned ask earlier.

nodes.label: ToolRegistry.run
nodes.detail: Read-only allow, acceptEdits edit allow, and default ask after confirmation all eventually reach the real tool function.

nodes.label: error ToolResult
nodes.detail: A write request in plan mode returns an error observation, nudging the model to analyze read-only or ask the user to switch modes.

diagrams.label: Human + background
diagrams.title: User questions and background bash are harness branches
diagrams.summary: ask_user_question opens a menu and constructs ToolResult inside the harness; bash(background=True) starts a subprocess via bg_manager and writes output to .bg files for later polling.

nodes.label: ask_user_question
nodes.detail: When the model needs the user's decision, it proposes ask_user_question. It is a first-class tool, not a slash command.

nodes.label: permission gate
nodes.detail: ask_user_question is not in the read-only allowlist, but its ask branch is a choice menu rather than a y/N confirmation.

nodes.label: prompt_single_choice
nodes.detail: The harness pauses the Agent Loop, waits for 1/2/3, then sends the selected label back as the tool result.

nodes.label: ToolResult
nodes.detail: ask_user_question's entire job is to collect an answer, so this branch does not call tools.run.

nodes.label: bash background=True
nodes.detail: Background bash still passes through the same permission engine; background=True is not a permission bypass.

nodes.label: permission gate
nodes.detail: Long commands such as sleep or npm run build follow the same policy as synchronous bash: dangerous commands deny, normal commands ask.

nodes.label: start_background
nodes.detail: The tool starts a subprocess and returns immediately, so the Agent Loop is not blocked. stdout and stderr stream to .bg.

nodes.label: .bg/<id>.out/.err
nodes.detail: The observation gives the model a background_id, pid, and output paths. The files may be empty at first.

nodes.label: later bash cat
nodes.detail: The model later calls bash("cat .bg/<id>.out") or bash("kill <pid>") to inspect or stop the background job.

diagrams.label: Background lifecycle
diagrams.title: What a full background-task lifecycle looks like
diagrams.summary: Production background bash is not just starting a thread. It is a task lifecycle: the same Bash tool enters background mode, the harness registers a task, streams output, returns a task id immediately, and later reads, cancels, or notifies through that id.

nodes.label: Bash tool_use
nodes.detail: The production shape is not a separate bg tool. It is the same Bash tool with a background flag. The teaching version calls this background=True.

nodes.label: Permission gate
nodes.detail: Background mode does not bypass permission. Dangerous commands still deny first, normal commands still ask; the difference is that a confirmed command does not block until completion.

nodes.label: LocalShellTask
nodes.detail: A full lifecycle needs a task object: task id, process handle, output path, and running status. Without this registry, status, cancel, and completion notifications are hard to build.

nodes.label: task output file
nodes.detail: Output streams into a task file. The model does not need to wait for process exit; it can read the file later. Day 5 approximates this with .bg/<id>.out and .bg/<id>.err.

nodes.label: ToolResult
nodes.detail: A successful background launch immediately returns the task id and output path, so the model knows what to inspect later instead of blocking on the current tool call.

nodes.label: read / cancel
nodes.detail: The full system uses the task id for later actions: read output, inspect status, or cancel. The official shape does not expose separate bg_read/bg_cancel tools; Day 5 uses cat output file or kill pid.

nodes.label: completion notification
nodes.detail: A complete lifecycle can notify the model when a task finishes. Day 5 does not implement a notification queue; it keeps the minimal model-pulls-output path.
